# Atlas (`@steel-dev/atlas`)

> Deep research as a TypeScript library. One recursive agent primitive under a
> tree-shared USD budget: a lead agent plans, spawns research and verify
> subagents (or answers inline — topology emerges, it is never configured),
> writes verbatim-quoted claims into one shared ledger, and a final binding
> pass machine-checks every cited sentence of the report. Runs on the Vercel
> AI SDK 6, so any model provider works.

The value is the verification pipeline: every claim carries a verbatim quote
string-checked against the stored source text, central claims are adversarially
verified by independent lens voters, and the drafted report is re-checked
sentence by sentence (citation binding) before it is returned.

This file is the agent-facing brief — a compact reference for coding agents
building on Atlas. Full prose lives in README.md.

## Install

```bash
npm install @steel-dev/atlas ai @ai-sdk/anthropic   # + the provider you use
```

Keys (env): a model provider key (`ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` or `OPENAI_API_KEY`).
Optional: `TAVILY_API_KEY` / `EXA_API_KEY` / `BRAVE_API_KEY` (search adapters;
falls back to the model's native server-side search) and `STEEL_API_KEY`
(escalates blocked/JS-rendered pages through Steel).

## SDK — core API

```ts
import { Atlas } from "@steel-dev/atlas";
import { anthropic } from "@ai-sdk/anthropic";

const atlas = new Atlas({ model: anthropic("claude-fable-5") });
const result = await atlas.research("What's changing in browser automation?");
result.report;              // cited Markdown report
result.citations;           // [{ sentenceSpan, claimId, sourceId, quote, verified }]
result.claims.confirmed;    // survived the full adversarial panel
result.claims.screened;     // passed the cheap screening check only
result.claims.contested;    // sources disagree — surfaced, not buried
result.findings;            // statements + confidence + claim/source ids
result.stats;               // costUSD, tokens per role, agentsSpawned, singleAgent, …
```

`new Atlas(config)` fields:

- `model` (required) — an AI SDK model instance.
- `models?` — per-role overrides: `{ lead?, research?, verify?, extract?, write? }`.
  A cheap `extract`/`verify` model stretches the budget much further.
- `effort?` — `"fast" | "balanced" | "deep" | "max"` (default balanced); sets
  the budget envelope and spawn depth/breadth caps.
- `budget?` — `{ maxUSD?, maxDurationMs?, maxSources? }`; `maxUSD` is the hard
  ceiling and the only test-time-compute knob.
- `search?` — `tavily()` / `exa()` / `brave()` / `nativeModelSearch({model})`,
  one or an array (results are rank-fused).
- `fetch?` — fetch chain, default `[basicFetch()]` + `steel()` when keyed.
- `store?` — `memoryStore()` (default) or `fileStore(dir)`; enables resume.
- `instructions?` — domain framing appended to the lead agent's system prompt.
- `tools?` — `Record<string, ResearchTool>` of domain tools (see below).
- `pricing?` — per-model USD pricing overrides; `safety?` — SSRF/quarantine knobs.

Calls:

- `atlas.research(question, opts?)` → `Promise<ResearchResult>`.
- `atlas.research(question, { output: { kind: "structured", schema } })` →
  adds `result.structured` (typed, filled from verified claims) and
  `result.structuredBasis` (field path → citations + reasoning).
- `atlas.start(question, opts?)` → run handle: `run.events()` (async iterable
  of typed events: `plan.updated`, `agent.spawned`, `claim.verified`,
  `report.delta`, `budget.warning`, …), `run.result()`, `run.cancel()`,
  `run.pause()`, `run.status()`.
- `Atlas.resume(runId, config)` — replay the journal from `config.store`;
  completed model calls are served from the journal at zero cost.

## Custom tools — the extension point for domain researchers

A tool's results become citable sources: anything passed to `ctx.addSource` is
extracted into ledger claims, verified, and cited exactly like a fetched page.

```ts
import { Atlas, researchTool } from "@steel-dev/atlas";
import { anthropic } from "@ai-sdk/anthropic";
import { z } from "zod";

const atlas = new Atlas({
  model: anthropic("claude-fable-5"),
  instructions: "You are a clinical evidence analyst. Prefer peer-reviewed sources.",
  tools: {
    pubmedSearch: researchTool({
      description: "Search PubMed. Each result becomes a citable source.",
      inputSchema: z.object({ query: z.string(), limit: z.number().default(5) }),
      execute: async ({ query, limit }, ctx) => {
        const studies = await pubmed.search(query, { limit, signal: ctx.signal });
        for (const s of studies)
          ctx.addSource({ url: s.url, title: s.title, content: s.abstract });
        return studies.map((s) => `- ${s.title} — ${s.url}`).join("\n");
      },
    }),
  },
});
```

`ToolContext`: `addSource({ url, title?, content })`, `log(message)`, `signal?`.
Custom tools are reachable from the lead and research agents, never from
verifiers (read-only verification is an invariant).

## Building a domain researcher

A domain researcher is a small module: `new Atlas({ model, instructions, tools })`,
optionally with a structured `output` per run. Compose domain `researchTool`s and
let the verification pipeline do the rest.

## Notes for agents

- Don't bypass or reimplement the verification pipeline — it is the product.
  Tools only *add sources*; extraction, verification, and binding stay in the
  engine.
- Contract types (`ResearchResult`, `ResearchClaim`, `Citation`, `RunStats`,
  `ResearchEvent`, …) are exported from the package. Import them; never copy
  or fork them.
- Any model the AI SDK supports works; install its provider package and pass
  the model instance to `model` (and per-role overrides via `models`).
- Budget is the knob. Don't add loops or retries around `research()` to "try
  harder" — raise `effort` or `budget.maxUSD` instead.
